The goal is to understand all the methods and techniques of how people build a modern and analytical database system.
You'll learn how to write good code, how to write a good report, how to write a document and testing plans for that code.

There will be a little bit of code reviews during the semester and then obviously working on a large code base.
Maybe not entirely true for these projects in the beginning, but since we're doing semester-long projects this year, your code base is going to get quite large by the end.
We're really focusing here on studio topics. We're not going to be rehashing stuff that we covered in the intro class or going through the textbook.
It's really about looking at the latest research paper and the latest literature on building a modern system and then seeing how we can apply that to our...
At its core, the overarching model of the database system will be trying to build a developed...

So on that topic, these are the main ideas or sections we're going to cover throughout the semester.

So first we're going to talk about what the data actually should look like, the data formats, how we're doing coding compression in such a way that it will speed up data access and but also reduce the footprint of our databases.
We've been talking about different methods to accelerate query processing.
So doing vectorized query execution or co-generation and query compilation, we had to take a query plan, physical query plan and execute it most efficiently on the data.
Then we'll look at a lot of larger view of the system of how we handle scheduling within a single query itself and over across the entire workload.
How do we do...
We had to run journalism sufficiently, how do we handle network protocols between the nodes and between the nodes and the client.
We spent a lot of time talking about query optimization because again, it doesn't matter how fast we've got our database system, if our query plan is sub optimal, just garbage.
Then all the stuff we're going to do before then doesn't actually matter. That's wasted time.
Then we're going to spend a significant third of the lecture, a quarter of the lecture, a semester talking about or looking at real implementations of data systems.
